title: The Madness That Gives Wings — Ficino, Plato, and Divine Enthusiasm
author: Plato / Marsilio Ficino

body.text: Ficino'stheory of inspiration rests on adangerous distinction. One kind of madness breaks the soul downward. Another kind opens the ordinary ego so agodlike power can enter. Plato calls this divine mania. The inspired soul is seized by something beyond its usual range. The daimonic arrives as winged disturbance.

There is also amadness which is adivine gift, and the source of the chiefest blessings granted to men. For prophecy is amadness, and the prophetess at Delphi and the priestesses at Dodona when they have been mad have conferred great benefits on Hellas, but when sane have done little or nothing. And Icould also tell of how the Sibyl and others who have been inspired have foretold many things to many men and enabled them to see the future.

Plato'sclaim startles. The gods give their best gifts through madness, through being possessed by something larger than yourself. Ficino receives this as apsychology of inspiration. Poetry becomes possession; prophecy and sacred music follow the same law. The soul becomes most itself when it is drawn beyond itself.

Let our figure be of acomposite nature — apair of winged horses and acharioteer. Now the winged horses of the gods are both of noble breed, but of the others one is noble and of noble breed, and the other is the opposite. The charioteer of the human soul drives apair.

The chariot gives Ficino amap of inner governance. The soul is composite. One horse pulls upward, another downward. The charioteer must learn the art of direction. Beauty alone can intoxicate. Discipline alone can harden. The chariot must be both moved and governed.

body.caption: The chariot of the soul — reason, noble impulse, and appetite in a single yoke.

body.text: But he who is newly initiated, who beheld much of the vision, when he sees agodlike face or form which is agood expression of beauty, first shudders and feels something of the old awe; then he looks upon it and reverences it as agod, and if he were not afraid of being thought mad he would offer sacrifice to his beloved as to aholy image.

Beauty strikes the soul as anamnesis — aremembering. The beautiful face becomes agate. The soul trembles because beauty loosens the ordinary arrangement of perception. The beloved form is the shock by which the soul recalls what it saw before embodiment. This is Ficino'seros charged with wings.
